config.title: What contributes to the slowdown of global progress?
config.abstract: While the world has witnessed decades of remarkable development progress, this era of advancement is now facing a critical juncture. Global development is currently proceeding at its slowest pace in 75 years, a widespread slowdown impacting crucial areas from poverty reduction and life expectancy to education and women's empowerment. This stall carries immense consequences, leaving millions behind. Explore the data to understand this slowdown and what recent data reveals about progress in countries around the world.
config.citation: Lambrechts, M, Mahler D G, Thudt, A, and Wadhwa, D. 2026. “Global progress is grinding to a halt.” In Atlas of  Global Development 2026, edited by A F Pirlea, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, D Wadhwa and M Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
config.credits: Daniel Gerszon Mahler and Divyanshi Wadhwa

content.text: The world is developing at its slowest pace in 75 years

For 75 years, global development moved decisively forward, lifting millions out of poverty and delivering major gains in health, education, and other vital areas. That progress is no longer assured. Development is now advancing at its slowest pace in generations, marking a clear break from the steady improvements that defined progress up until now and raising urgent questions about the world’s ability to sustain past gains.

Getting a long-run picture of progress in global development requires data spanning decades. For a handful of key development indicators, data exists dating back to 1950: poverty, life expectancy, years of schooling, women's political empowerment, electricity supply, and carbon intensity of the economy.[reference: mahler2026] Due to their long time span, these indicators will be the focus of this story. As will be shown later, the dire outlook they suggest about global progress generalizes to 20 indicators with shorter data availability.

Comparing progress across diverse development indicators over time is inherently difficult, but it can be done using a common benchmark. One approach is to measure a country’s progress relative to the pace at which the typical country advanced over the past 75 years, allowing today’s performance to be evaluated against long‑run historical norms. For example, if it takes the typical country 10 years to decrease poverty rates from 20% to 15% and a country did this in 5 years, it can be said to have moved twice as fast as the typical experience. If it achieved the feat in 10 years, it moved at the typical speed – a speed of one – while if it took 20 years, it moved at a speed of a half (see an interactive visual explanation of how these speed scores are constructed here).

content.title: The global speed of progress is falling in many domains
content.subtitle: Global average speed of progress, 1950-2024
content.visdescription: This visualization shows the speed of progress across six development indicators from 1950 to 2025.

scenes.text: If we calculate the speed of progress across all countries and average these year by year, we can get a picture of the changing pace of development over the last 75 years. Here this is done for extreme poverty rates.
We can do the same for multiple indicators and see how they compare.

The pace of progress changes annually and smoothening the data helps distinguish patterns from noise. For life expectancy and electricity supply, the pace of progress is the slowest it has been in 75 years. For education and poverty, the current pace is the slowest we have seen since the 1960s or the 1990s. For the first time since 1950, progress in women’s political empowerment is reversing. Only for CO2 emissions per GDP is progress faster than in past decades.
If we overlay the six indicators and calculate the average speed across indicators, we get a picture of how the pace of global development has changed over the last 75 years.
With this set of indicators, the global speed of progress has declined since around 2010, and is today at its lowest point since 1950.

content.text: This slowdown is widespread across regions. In every region except South Asia, the pace of progress is now the slowest observed since 1950.  The consequences are far reaching. We can measure the impact of the decline by calculating the gap between where we are today and where we would have been if the pace of progress had not slowed down. If the world had maintained its pace of progress from 2010, there would be 150 million fewer people living in extreme poverty today, nearly a year higher life expectancy, and a 15% increase in the index of women’s empowerment.

The slowdown is evident across a wide range of development indicators extending beyond the six chosen here, which were selected partly based on data availability in 1950. Progress is at its slowest on record for many key outcomes, including stunting, undernourishment, under‑5 mortality, GNI per capita, access to clean cooking fuels, cereal yield, immunization coverage (DPT and measles), employment share in services, and access to safe drinking water. For several other indicators (including rural electricity access, basic water and sanitation, safely managed sanitation, and vulnerable employment) progress has recently slowed. Only three of the twenty indicators examined show accelerating progress: gross tertiary enrollment, mobile subscriptions, and internet use.
Explore progress across countries and indicators

You can explore this approach to looking at progress applied to 13 key indicators from the Atlas of Global Development and check how your country has performed relative to others over the last 10 years.

content.explanation2: in one country in one year
content.fallback: No progress speed data for this goal. Switch goal to see progress speed.

scenes.text: In the Atlas, each square represents a data point for a particular country, year and development indicator. You can change the selected indicator in the dropdown in the top-left of the screen. ↖️
Here we see  the [emphasis: latest available values] for the selected indicator across countries, with larger squares signifying higher values.
Let’s see [emphasis: which countries are doing well] in this indicator. Their squares are filled completely. Smaller squares represent a bigger gap to the leading countries.

Current outcomes do not reveal how much progress has been made. In this illustrative chart, the dark triangle represents the latest observed value, while the transparent triangle represents the level that would have been reached under the typical pace of progress since 2015. When the dark triangle lies ahead of the transparent one, progress has exceeded the typical path.

Let’s look at a [emphasis: slow], a [emphasis: typical], and a [emphasis: fast][emphasis: country ]in comparison. For the [emphasis: typical ]country, the typical progress (the transparent triangle) roughly reflects the country’s actual outcome, while for the [emphasis: slow] country, the typical progress is more advanced than where the country is today.

Now, let’s [emphasis: compare the speed of progress of all countries]. The countries to the left made the least progress – often reversing outcomes – while the countries to the right made the fastest progress.
If we order the countries by where they were in 2015,  we see that progress and setbacks are experienced at all levels of development.

Ordered by their latest value, one sees that some countries that were far apart 10 years ago now have similar outcomes. See how this carries through in other indicators used in the Atlas of Global Development Indicators by changing the view. You can bring up details by hovering over the countries.

references.mahler2026: Mahler, D G, Serajuddin, U, Wadhwa, D and Yonzan, N. 2026. The World Is Developing at Its Slowest Pace in 75 Years.
